children.text: CL exhibits a pattern similar to Snow Tha Product's regarding copula use. Like Snow, CL is not wrapping her entire verse in English, but within the lyrical code-switches from Korean into English, you can hear traces of the language used to first create and perform hip-hop in Harlem. CL, despite being from South Korea, also uses
(e.g., bare gerunds "flowing," and "looking,") alongside standardized copula productions (e.g., "that

"). Additionally, CL also makes use of specific AAVE words popularized through hip-hop's commercialization, including "real" to mean authentic and "fly" to mean cool or desirable. Together, CL offers another example of how incorporating artistically marked structures into lyrics with associations in differing demographics does not necessarily index place or dialect. Rather, the use of these features creatively and strategically in the situated practice of rap functions to align the artistic affiliation of the performer within their intended genre-world by attuning to how said forms intertextually relate to other performances/performers/genres/identities, etc.
